modules.title: Trailer Basics
modules.description: What trailers are, how they work, and how rentals fit in. Foundation knowledge before we dive into Neighbors Trailer specifically.
learning_objectives: Identify common types of trailers and their primary uses
Understand basic hitching and towing terminology
Explain why people rent trailers instead of buying
Recognize key safety and legal considerations for towing

sections.title: What is a trailer?
sections.content_md:
A trailer is an unpowered vehicle towed by a powered one. The key distinction: trailers don't move on their own. They're attached to a truck, SUV, or car via a hitch and pulled along. They show up in everyday life everywhere — moving across the country, hauling a boat to the lake, picking up lumber from Home Depot, transporting a horse, taking a side-by-side to the dunes.

The US trailer rental market is large because most people don't need a trailer often enough to justify owning one. Buying a $5,000 utility trailer to use twice a year doesn't make sense. Renting one for $50 a day does. That gap is the entire reason peer-to-peer trailer rental marketplaces like Neighbors Trailer exist.
key_points: Trailers don't have their own engine — they're towed
Most people need a trailer occasionally, not regularly
Renting is cheaper than buying for occasional use

sections.title: Common types of trailers
sections.content_md:
Trailers fall into rough categories based on what they haul. As a CSR you don't need to be an expert, but recognizing these by name will save you on every other call:

**Utility trailers** — open, flat-bedded, often with low side rails. The most common rental. Used for moving, yard waste, furniture, ATVs, anything weather doesn't matter for.

**Enclosed trailers** — fully boxed in with a door. Used when cargo can't get wet or seen. Movers, contractors, anyone hauling tools or valuables.

**Car haulers** — long, low, designed to carry a single vehicle. Two ramps at the back. Used for moving non-running cars, race cars, project vehicles.

**Flatbed trailers** — flat platform with no walls or roof. For oversized loads — equipment, building materials, hay bales.

**Dump trailers** — like a small dump truck bed but towed. Hydraulic lift to dump dirt, gravel, debris. Common for landscaping and demo work.

**Horse / livestock trailers** — designed for transporting animals. Often divided into stalls. Some have living quarters on the front for owners.

**Tow dollies** — minimal trailers with just two wheels and ramps; tow a car by lifting only its front wheels.

**Boat trailers** — specialized cradle for hauling boats. Skinny, with rollers or bunks shaped for the hull.

**Motorcycle trailers** — small, often with wheel chocks to lock bikes upright.

Neighbors Trailer's listings span all of these categories. Knowing the difference between a 'flatbed' and a 'utility' helps you understand what the renter is asking about when they call.
key_points: Utility = open, the most common rental
Enclosed = boxed in, for valuables or weather protection
Car hauler = for vehicles
Specialty types: dump, horse, boat, motorcycle, tow dolly

sections.title: Hitching and towing basics
sections.content_md:
Trailers attach to tow vehicles via a **hitch**. The two most common types you'll hear about:

**Ball hitch** — by far the most common. A metal ball mounted on the tow vehicle's hitch receiver. The trailer has a coupler that locks down over the ball. Comes in three standard sizes: 1-7/8", 2", and 2-5/16". The ball size MUST match the coupler — wrong size is a major safety issue.

**Gooseneck / fifth-wheel** — used for heavier trailers (large livestock, big flatbeds). Mounts in the bed of a pickup truck rather than the rear bumper. Distributes weight better; allows heavier loads.

**Light plug** — every trailer with brake lights/turn signals also plugs into the tow vehicle's electrical system. Two common types: **4-pin** (basic — running, brake, turn) and **7-pin** (4-pin functions plus electric brakes, reverse, charging). Renters routinely call confused because their truck's plug doesn't match the trailer's plug.

**Tow capacity** — every vehicle has a maximum weight it can safely tow, listed in the owner's manual. The renter is responsible for knowing their vehicle's tow capacity. As a CSR, never quote a tow capacity for a renter's specific vehicle — that's a liability you don't want.

**Trailer brakes** — heavier trailers have their own brakes that activate via a brake controller installed in the tow vehicle. If a renter is asking about a 7-pin plug or 'do I need a brake controller,' the listing has electric brakes.
key_points: Ball hitch sizes: 1-7/8", 2", 2-5/16" — must match
Gooseneck = heavy-duty, mounts in pickup bed
Light plugs: 4-pin (basic) vs 7-pin (with electric brakes)
Never quote tow capacity for someone's specific vehicle

media.caption: Ball hitch sizes — 1-7/8", 2", 2-5/16". Must match the trailer coupler exactly.

media.alt: Gooseneck trailer mounted in a pickup bed
media.caption: Gooseneck hitch — mounts in the bed of a pickup truck, used for heavier loads.

sections.title: Why people rent
sections.content_md:
Understanding the renter's mindset helps every conversation. Common reasons people rent rather than buy:

**Moving** — the largest category. People moving across town or across the country. Often first-time trailer users; they need extra hand-holding.

**One-off projects** — landscaping a yard, hauling debris from a remodel, picking up bulky furniture, moving a riding mower.

**Recreation** — hauling ATVs, side-by-sides, boats, motorcycles to a destination. Weekend warriors.

**Trying before buying** — people considering a trailer purchase rent first to see if the size works for them.

**Their own trailer is unavailable** — broken, sold, lent out. They need a temporary replacement.

For owners on Neighbors Trailer, the equation is the same in reverse. They own a trailer, use it occasionally, and want to defray ownership costs by renting it out the rest of the time. Many owners are landscapers, contractors, or hobbyists whose trailer sits unused most weeks.

This isn't 'random rental income' for them — for many owners it's a real chunk of their monthly cash flow. When you're handling an owner's payout problem, that context matters.
key_points: Moving and one-off projects are the bulk of rentals
Renters are often first-time trailer users
For owners, payouts are real income — not pocket money

sections.title: Safety and legal essentials
sections.content_md:
You don't need to be a tow expert as a CSR, but you should recognize the most common safety/legal issues that come up:

**Driver's license requirements** — CDL requirements depend on **local laws and regulations** and vary by state and trailer weight class. Renters who think they need a CDL should be directed to **check with their local DMV.** As a CSR, don't quote a specific weight cutoff over the phone — the rules vary, and the safe answer is "check with your local DMV." The MVR check that runs at booking covers most license-validity issues, but it doesn't replace the renter knowing what their state requires.

**Damage protection** — Neighbors Trailer provides NT Protect / the Protection Package on every booking — a built-in damage protection product, **not insurance**, that covers accidental damage during the rental. **Tire damage is case-by-case** — see Module 6 for the full rule (tire condition + cause determine responsibility; not a flat exclusion). Intentional damage and theft due to renter negligence are excluded.

**Trailer registration & plates** — owner's responsibility. Every trailer on the platform must be legally registered in the owner's state.

**Securing the load** — the renter's responsibility. Nothing falling out, nothing flying off, properly tied down. NT does not inspect this; the owner verbally walks renters through it at pickup.

**Speed and route** — towing changes everything about driving. Stopping distance is longer. Turns are wider. Some highways and bridges have weight restrictions. Most rental trailers have a recommended max speed (typically 55-65 mph).

When a renter asks 'do I need a CDL?' — the right answer is "check your local DMV; the rules vary by state and trailer weight." Don't give them a number.
key_points: CDL requirements vary by local law — direct renters to their DMV, do NOT quote weight cutoffs
NT Protect covers accidental damage; tire damage is case-by-case (Module 6)
Securing the load is the renter's responsibility
Towing changes stopping distance, turning radius, max speed

quiz.question: Which type of trailer is fully enclosed and used when cargo needs protection from weather or theft?
quiz.explanation: Enclosed trailers are fully boxed in with walls, roof, and a door — used by movers, contractors, and anyone hauling valuables or weather-sensitive cargo.

quiz.question: What's the difference between a 4-pin and a 7-pin trailer light plug?
options: 4-pin is for cars, 7-pin is for trucks
4-pin handles basic lights only; 7-pin adds electric brakes, reverse, and charging
4-pin is older, 7-pin is the new standard
quiz.explanation: 4-pin handles running lights, brake lights, and turn signals. 7-pin adds electric trailer brakes, reverse signal, and a charging line. Heavier trailers with their own brakes need a 7-pin connection.

quiz.question: Should a CSR quote a renter's specific tow capacity over the phone?
options: Yes, if you can look it up online
Yes, if the trailer manufacturer's site lists it
No — tow capacity depends on the renter's vehicle, and the renter is responsible for knowing it
quiz.explanation: Tow capacity is specific to the renter's vehicle and is the renter's responsibility. Quoting it creates liability if it's wrong. Always direct them to their owner's manual.

quiz.question: An owner asks if a tire blowout from road debris during a rental is covered by NT Protect. What's the most accurate answer?
options: Tires can be covered like any other damage — but the renter pays the $500 deductible and most single-tire damage doesn't exceed that threshold
Tire damage is permanently excluded from NT Protect coverage
All tire damage is the renter's responsibility regardless of tire condition
Tire damage is only covered if the renter caused it
quiz.explanation: Tires are covered the same as any other damage under NT Protect — there's no special tire exclusion. What makes tire claims rare is the $500 deductible. A single tire usually costs less than $500 to replace, so the damage falls below the deductible threshold. That means renter or owner pays directly without filing a Protection Package claim — but it's NOT because tires are excluded. If multiple tires are damaged or the repair cost exceeds $500 (e.g., curb impact damaging two tires plus rim), it can be filed as a Protection Package claim. Don't tell customers 'tire damage isn't covered' — tell them how the deductible works.

quiz.question: A renter calls asking if they need a CDL to rent a standard trailer. What's the correct CSR answer?
options: Yes, always — every trailer requires a CDL
No — Class C is sufficient for trailers under 10,000 lb GVWR
CDL requirements depend on local laws and regulations — direct them to check with their local DMV
Only if they're towing across state lines
quiz.explanation: CDL requirements vary by state and trailer weight class. Don't quote a specific cutoff over the phone — direct the renter to their local DMV. The MVR check at booking covers license validity but doesn't replace the renter knowing local rules.

modules.title: Platform Foundations
modules.description: Overview of NT, the renter and owner roles, the booking lifecycle, and core platform terminology.
learning_objectives: Explain what Neighbors Trailer is in one sentence
Distinguish between Renter and Owner roles
Walk through the full booking lifecycle from search to payout

sections.title: What is Neighbors Trailer?
sections.content_md:
Neighbors Trailer (NT) is a peer-to-peer trailer rental marketplace. **Owners list their trailers** and **renters book them**. NT does not own any of the trailers on the platform.

What NT actually provides:
- Payment processing
- Identity verification (Clear)
- MVR (driver record) checks
- GPS tracking on rentals
- NT Protect — the Protection Package, a built-in damage protection product (NOT insurance) that covers accidental damage
- Dispute resolution

If someone asks 'aren't you just like Turo for trailers?' — yes, that's a fine analogy. The model is the same: a two-sided marketplace where the platform handles trust, payments, and safety so that owners and renters can transact directly.
key_points: Peer-to-peer marketplace — NT does not own trailers
Two roles: Owner and Renter
NT handles payments, verification, GPS, Protection Package, disputes

sections.title: The Booking Lifecycle
sections.content_md:
Every rental follows the same nine-step lifecycle. Memorize this — most CSR calls map back to a specific step.

1. Renter searches and finds a trailer
2. Renter requests a booking
3. Renter completes ID verification (Clear) and MVR
4. Owner accepts (or rejects) the booking
5. Pickup address and owner phone shared automatically once accepted
6. Renter picks up trailer; owner 'starts' the rental
7. Rental period — renter has the trailer
8. Renter returns trailer; owner 'ends' the rental
9. Reviews left, deposit refunded, owner payout issued

Key friction points to know cold:
- The pickup address is **not** sent in a separate email — it appears on the booking page after acceptance.
- Booking confirmation is **not instant** — it requires owner acceptance.
- The rental is only 'started' and 'ended' by the owner clicking those buttons in their account.
key_points: 9 steps from search to payout
Pickup address shows after owner accepts — auto
Owner manually starts and ends each rental

sections.title: Core Terminology
sections.content_md:
These terms come up in nearly every conversation. Use them confidently.

- **NT Protect / Protection Package** — built-in damage protection covering accidental damage during rentals. **Not insurance** — it's a damage protection product, not an insurance policy. Tire damage and intentional damage are excluded.
- **Toll Program** — opt-in for owners. Auto-tracks tolls during rentals and bills the renter.
- **My Reservations** — owner-side feature for managing their bookings.
- **MVR** — Motor Vehicle Record check, required for renters before booking is allowed.
- **Clear** — third-party ID verification provider used by NT.
- **Pending booking** — a booking the owner has not yet accepted.
key_points: NT Protect = damage protection (not insurance), with exclusions
Toll Program = owner opt-in
MVR + Clear = required renter verifications

sections.title: Multi-Booking — Allowed, but Each One Is Real
sections.content_md:
**Renters CAN book multiple trailers at the same time.** The platform doesn't restrict this. Each booking is independent.

**The catch:** if one booking falls through (rejected, doesn't work out), the **standard cancellation policy applies normally to the others.** Each booking is its own contract.

**Do NOT teach trainees the "back-up booking" workaround.** It used to come up as a way to hedge: "book two trailers in case one falls through, then cancel the one you don't need." That advice is wrong because:
- Both bookings are real, with real money on the renter's card
- Cancelling the unused one falls under the cancellation refund schedule
- Owners on the cancelled-side end up with a wasted hold on their calendar

**Right framing for a renter on the fence:** "Each booking you place is a real reservation with money on the line. If you're unsure about one, message the owner with questions before booking — don't book a second one as backup."
key_points: Renters can book multiple trailers at once — each booking is independent
Cancellation policy applies normally if they cancel one to keep the other
Do NOT teach the "back-up booking" workaround — it costs the renter money and burns owners' calendars

sections.title: The Two Required Verifications — Every Booking
sections.content_md:
Before a renter can book, they must complete **two** verifications:

1. **ID verification via Clear** — third-party identity check
2. **MVR (Motor Vehicle Record)** — driver record check

**Critical:** ID verification is required on **EVERY booking**, not just the first. Not on a schedule. Not just when the system flags something. Every. Single. Booking. This is a notable simplification from older curriculum that taught a more complex "first booking only" or "every 90 days" schedule. Tell the renter cleanly: "You'll re-verify each time you book."

**Minimum age to rent: 21.** A renter under 21 cannot book on the platform. This is a hard cutoff.

A standard **Class C license** is sufficient for renting a standard trailer. **No CDL required.** Customers often think a CDL is needed and it isn't — clear that up early in the call.

If either verification fails, the booking is blocked. There is no manual override path that a CSR can take. A failed MVR is escalated; a refused Clear is handled by re-explaining the purpose.
key_points: ID verification required on EVERY booking — not just the first
Minimum age to rent: 21 (hard cutoff)
Both Clear and MVR must pass before booking
Standard Class C license is enough — no CDL
CSR cannot override either rejection

sections.title: Handling Clear Refusals
sections.content_md:
The most common reasons renters refuse Clear:
- Privacy concerns ('I don\'t want to share my ID')
- Technical issues with the Clear flow
- They don\'t have the right ID on hand

What to say:
- Clear is industry-standard and used by airlines and stadiums.
- NT does **not** store your ID image.
- Verification protects both renters and owners — it's how we keep bad actors off the platform.

Don't argue. Acknowledge their concern, explain the purpose, and let them decide. If they still refuse, the booking simply can't proceed.
key_points: Clear is industry-standard — name-drop airlines/stadiums
NT does not store ID images
If they still refuse, booking can't proceed

sections.title: MVR Rejections
sections.content_md:
MVR rejections come from things like:
- License suspensions
- Multiple recent moving violations
- License expired
- License type doesn't qualify

**A CSR cannot override an MVR rejection. Escalate.**

When handling the call:
1. Acknowledge their frustration ('I hear you, that's not the answer you were hoping for')
2. Explain that MVR is a third-party check and NT cannot manually override it
3. Offer to escalate so the team can review the specific reason
4. Don't promise a reversal
key_points: CSR cannot override — escalate every time
Be empathetic but don't promise an outcome
Explain MVR is third-party

sections.title: Third-Party Drivers — The Gray Area
sections.content_md:
**This is a gray area, not a hard rule. Get the framing right.**

Officially: only the booking renter should be driving the trailer.

In practice: a third-party driver IS allowed **if the OWNER is OK with it.** It's a between-the-renter-and-the-owner agreement.

**The right thing to tell a renter** who asks if their friend / spouse / coworker can drive:
> "Officially the renter should be the driver, but if the owner approves a third-party driver, that's between you and the owner. Confirm with the owner before pickup."

**What NOT to say:**
- ❌ "Absolutely no third-party drivers." (That's wrong — it's owner discretion.)
- ❌ "Yes, anyone can drive." (Also wrong — the owner has to approve.)

The single most common version of this call: "My spouse and I share driving on long trips — can they drive too?" The right answer is the owner's call; coach the renter to message the owner before pickup.
key_points: Third-party driver = gray area, NOT a hard rule
Officially the renter drives; in practice owner can approve a third-party
Coach the renter to confirm with the owner before pickup
Never say "absolutely not" or "yes anyone can" — both are wrong

sections.title: Account Inactive vs. Deactivated — Triage
sections.content_md:
When a customer says "my account is inactive" or "my account is deactivated," the **first triage question** is: **which one?** The wording matters.

**Inactive** usually means a low-friction issue:
- Unverified email (most common)
- Missing required listing fields (owner side)
- Profile incomplete

**Deactivated** usually means one of two main causes:
1. **NT team deactivation due to policy violation** (compliance hold, fraud flag, repeat-offender pattern)
2. **User self-deactivation** (the user clicked deactivate in account settings)

**Reactivation flow** (for deactivated accounts):
1. User contacts NT support
2. Team reviews the deactivation reason
3. Team reactivates if appropriate

The CSR can't unilaterally reactivate a deactivated account — escalate to the team. For "inactive" cases, walk the user through the obvious fixes (resend verification email, complete profile fields).

**Right opening question on these calls:** "Is the account showing inactive or deactivated? The wording on the screen matters — they're different paths."
key_points: First question: inactive or deactivated? Wording matters.
Inactive → unverified email, missing fields, incomplete profile
Deactivated → NT team policy violation OR user self-deactivation
Reactivation = user contacts support → team reviews → team reactivates if appropriate
CSR cannot unilaterally reactivate — escalate

sections.title: The Booking Flow (Get the Order Right)
sections.content_md:
**The correct booking order is:**

1. **Renter searches** for a trailer
2. **Renter completes ID verification** (during the booking process — Clear + MVR)
3. **Renter pays** and submits the booking
4. **Owner accepts** the booking and coordinates pickup

**This is important:** there is **no "owner accepts → THEN renter pays" flow.** Payment and ID verification happen at booking submission. The owner's acceptance comes after, with money already on the renter's card.

Reminder from Module 2: **ID verification is required on EVERY booking** — not just the first. So step 2 happens every time the renter books.

**Owner-acceptance timing:** NT does not have a hard SLA on owner response. If the renter is anxious about the wait, reassure them most owners accept within a few hours. If it's been substantially longer with no response, you can gently nudge the owner via the messaging system or escalate to a manager based on context.

While waiting, the renter can also look at alternative trailers (different listings) — see the multi-booking note in section 3-5 before suggesting they hold both.
key_points: Order: search → ID verify → pay → submit → owner accepts → coordinate
NO "owner accepts then renter pays" flow — payment + verification happen at submission
ID verification re-runs on every booking (not just first)
No hard SLA on owner response; reassure that most owners accept within a few hours; nudge or escalate based on context

sections.title: Date Changes — Bidirectional
sections.content_md:
**Either party — renter OR owner — can request a date change.** The other party must approve. The old curriculum framed this as renter-only-requests; that's wrong.

- **Renter requests** through their booking → owner approves or declines
- **Owner requests** through their reservation → renter approves or declines

If the other party declines, the booking stays on the original dates. The requesting party can then either keep the booking as-is or cancel under the cancellation policy (Module 7).

**There is no "force the change" path.** Both sides have to agree. CSR cannot push a date change through unilaterally.
key_points: Date change is bidirectional — either party can request
Other party must approve
Decline = booking stays on original dates; requester can cancel under policy
No CSR override on date changes

sections.title: No Shortening + Trailer Switches
sections.content_md:
**Renters CANNOT shorten an active or upcoming booking.** There is no "trim 2 days off" path through the system.

The only path to "less time" is **early return after pickup** — and that's the manual 80% partial refund path covered in Module 5/Module 7. To recap from those modules:
- Up to 80% refund of the unused-portion rent
- Owner must approve and authorize before ending the rental
- Refund is NOT automatic — owner discretion

A renter who wants to cut days has two options:
1. **Cancel and rebook** — taking the cancellation refund hit (Module 7 schedule)
2. **Proceed with the original booking and return early** — with no guarantee of refund

**Trailer switches:** A renter can switch to a different trailer **from the same owner** through the owner's 'My Reservations' feature. The owner facilitates it. Switching to a different owner requires cancelling and rebooking.
key_points: Renters cannot shorten a booking through the system
"Less time" = early return after pickup, with manual 80% refund path (owner discretion)
Two paths to cut days: cancel+rebook (refund hit) OR return early (no refund guarantee)
Same-owner trailer switch = via owner's My Reservations
Different-owner change = cancel + rebook

sections.title: Pickup Address & Owner No-Show
sections.content_md:
**Pickup address visibility:** the address is **hidden until the owner ACCEPTS the booking.** After acceptance:
- The address appears on the renter's booking page
- The owner sends the renter a message with the **full address + their contact info**

**Don't tell renters "you'll see it as soon as you book." That's wrong.** The address requires owner acceptance. If they're calling pre-acceptance asking where to pick up, the answer is "the owner hasn't accepted yet — once they do, the address shows up on your booking page and the owner will message you with their contact info."

Most "where's my address" calls are post-acceptance renters who haven't checked the booking page or didn't see the owner's message. Walk them to the booking details page.

**Owner no-show** (renter shows up at pickup time and the owner is a no-show):
- **Renter gets a full refund**
- **The owner faces consequences** — situational based on circumstances. Possibilities include:
  - Account review
  - Listing or account suspension
  - **30% fee** (same penalty structure as owner-initiated cancellation — see Module 4)

**Don't promise the renter "the owner will be banned."** Say something like: "You'll get a full refund, and the owner will face consequences which may include account review, suspension, or a fee, depending on the situation." Be specific about the renter's outcome (full refund) and vague about the owner's outcome (depends).
key_points: Pickup address hidden until owner accepts
After acceptance: address appears on booking page + owner sends a message with contact info
Owner no-show → renter gets full refund
Owner faces situational consequence (review, suspension, or 30% fee) — never promise "they'll be banned"

sections.title: Multi-Booking — Reference Module 1
sections.content_md:
**Renters can have multiple simultaneous bookings.** The platform doesn't restrict this. But each booking is independent and the **standard cancellation policy applies normally** if one is cancelled.

**Don't teach trainees to suggest "back-up booking" as a workaround.** Both bookings are real, with money on the card and a calendar block on the owner's side. The full lesson is in **Module 1, section 1-4** — refer trainees there for the rationale.

**The right framing for a renter on the fence:** "Each booking is a real reservation with money on the line. If you're unsure about one, message the owner with questions before booking — don't book a second one as backup."
key_points: Multi-booking allowed; each booking is independent
Cancellation policy applies normally if one is cancelled
Do NOT suggest "back-up booking" — full lesson in Module 1, §1-4

sections.title: Listing Approval & the VIN-Already-Exists Path
sections.content_md:
New listings go through approval. **Typical timing: 1-2 business hours when no issues are flagged. Up to 24-48 hours when something needs owner follow-up** (photos, dimensions, compliance items).

Common things that slow approval:
- Bad or low-quality photos
- Phone numbers, addresses, or company names visible in photos (will be removed)
- Missing dimensions (length, width, height, weight capacity)
- Hitch type not specified
- Description formatting issues

**VIN-already-exists workflow.** If an owner submits a listing with a VIN already on the platform, the listing is **NOT auto-rejected**. It goes to the review team for manual review. The team checks where that VIN already lives — an existing active listing, a prior account, an inactive listing — before approving the new submission.

When an owner calls about a VIN duplicate hold, the right framing is: "That VIN is already in our system, so the team is reviewing where it currently lives before approving your new listing. This is a manual review, not a rejection — they'll be in touch." Don't promise a timeline beyond "the team is on it."

Owners can list **as many trailers as they want.** No fleet cap. No special verification process for high-volume owners.
key_points: Listing approval: 1-2 business hours when clean; 24-48 hours when owner follow-up is needed
No phone numbers, addresses, or company names in photos
VIN already on platform → manual team review, not auto-reject
No fleet cap — owners can list unlimited trailers

sections.title: Listing Edits, Photos, and the 4-Photo Inspection Rule
sections.content_md:
**Owners can edit a listing at any time. No restrictions.** Pricing, photos, description, dimensions — all editable post-approval. Edits don't kick the listing back into approval (unless they trigger a flag like a phone number in a new photo).

**Pickup/return inspection — the only NT-mandated requirement is photos:**
- **4 photos at start of rental** (during pickup, when the owner starts the rental)
- **4 photos at end of rental** (during return, when the owner ends the rental)

That's the entire inspection NT mandates. Walkthrough, condition agreement, sign-offs — all of that is between the owner and renter. **NT does not physically inspect any trailer.** When an owner asks "do I need NT to inspect this?" the answer is no — the 4 photos are the inspection record.

**Why the photos matter:** they're the evidence the team uses if a damage claim is filed later. An owner who skips the start-of-rental photos and then files a DDC two days later has a much weaker case. Coach owners to take the photos every time, even when the rental looks routine.
key_points: Listings are editable anytime — pricing, photos, description, dimensions, all open
Inspection requirement = 4 photos at start + 4 photos at end. That is it.
NT does not physically inspect trailers
Skipping start-of-rental photos weakens any damage claim

sections.title: Start, End & Late Returns
sections.content_md:
After acceptance, the owner has two compliance actions tied to specific buttons in their account:

1. **Start the rental** when the renter picks up the trailer (and take the 4 start photos)
2. **End the rental** when the trailer is returned (and take the 4 end photos)

If an owner calls saying "I forgot to end the rental yesterday," walk them through ending it now and explain NT may need to manually adjust timing. End-it-now is always the answer.

**Late returns — owner's responsibility, not an NT auto-fee.** When a renter holds the trailer past the booked end time, NT does **NOT** auto-charge a late fee. The **owner has two options**:
- **Extend the rental** (with renter consent — see Module 7 for the extension flow)
- **File a Damage Deposit Deduction Claim (DDC)** and hold the deposit

The template sequence (Late Return Notice → Urgent Notice → Trailer Not Returned by Renter) still exists for communication and escalation, but those templates do not in themselves charge the renter. They're communication tools that lead toward DDC + escalation if the trailer isn't returned. Coach owners on the two-option choice — don't let them sit waiting for NT to magically charge a fee.
key_points: Owner manually starts and ends each rental
Late return = owner extends OR files a DDC. NT does not auto-charge late fees.
Templates communicate; they do not charge
Forgot to end? End it now.

sections.title: Owner Cancellation — 30% Fee, No With-Cause/Without-Cause
sections.content_md:
**The old "with cause vs. without cause" terminology is gone. Don't use it.**

**Owner cancellation policy:**
- If the owner cancels and it's **their fault** → owner pays a **30% cancellation fee**, deducted from their next payout.
- **Edge cases** — legitimate emergency, trailer broke unexpectedly, family emergency, etc. → the fee can be **waived at NT discretion**. The team reviews on a case-by-case basis.

**What to tell an owner who needs to cancel:**
1. "Owner-side cancellation carries a 30% fee that comes out of your next payout."
2. "If this is an emergency or something outside your control, mention that — the team can review whether a waiver applies."
3. Don't promise a waiver. Just open the door.

**What NOT to say:** "This is a without-cause cancel" / "This is a with-cause cancel." Those terms are dead. Anyone still using them is working from the old policy.
key_points: Owner cancellation = 30% fee from next payout
Waivable at NT discretion for legitimate emergencies / out-of-control situations
No with-cause/without-cause distinction — that terminology is dead
Open the door to a waiver review; don't promise one

sections.title: Owner Self-Serve Tools
sections.content_md:
Owners can self-serve the following without involving NT:
- **Edit any listing field anytime** (pricing, photos, description, dimensions, hitch type)
- **Block calendar dates**
- **Set or change rental rates**
- **Message renters** through the platform
- **Switch a renter to a different trailer** (via My Reservations) — same-owner only
- **List additional trailers** (no fleet cap)

Owners do **not** install hitches and should **not** quote tow capacity for the renter's vehicle. Tow capacity is the renter's responsibility based on their vehicle's documentation. Coach owners away from giving towing advice.
key_points: Self-serve: edit anytime, block dates, set rates, message, switch trailer, list more trailers
No fleet cap
Owners do not install hitches and should not quote tow capacity

modules.title: Payments, Fees & Payouts
modules.description: Renter fees, the cancellation refund schedule (75/50/25/0), refund timing (3-5 business days), 20% platform commission, owner payouts (1-3 business days, DDC hold), bank info failures, manual early-return refunds, chargebacks, and the Toll Program.
learning_objectives: Explain the renter fee breakdown and which fees are non-refundable

sections.title: Renter Fees, the Refund Schedule, and Refund Timing
sections.content_md:
When a renter books, the total includes:
- **Rent** (the trailer's daily rate)
- **Platform fee** (NT's service fee — non-refundable once accepted)
- **Tax** (non-refundable)
- **Deposit** (a hold, refunded after rental closes)
- **Protection Package** — NT Protect's built-in damage protection (not insurance), non-refundable once accepted

**The renter's card is charged when the booking is accepted by the owner**, not when the request is submitted. This trips up some renters.

**Renter-initiated cancellation refund schedule (auto-applied):**

| Time before booking start | Refund on rent |
|---|---|
| 72+ hours | 75% |
| 48-72 hours | 50% |
| 24-48 hours | 25% |
| Under 24 hours / same-day / no-show | 0% |

Platform fee, tax, and Protection Package remain non-refundable regardless of when the cancellation happens.

**Refund timing** — when a refund or deposit return is owed, it processes in **3-5 business days back to the renter's card**. This applies to cancellation refunds, early-return refunds, and deposit returns. (When a DDC is filed, the deposit return is held until the dispute window closes — see Module 6.)
key_points: Card charged at acceptance, not request
Cancellation refund: 75 / 50 / 25 / 0 at 72+ / 48-72 / 24-48 / under-24 hours
Platform fee, tax, Protection Package = non-refundable once accepted
Refund timing: 3-5 business days back to the card

sections.title: Owner Payouts — 80% Commission, 1-3 Business Days, DDC Hold
sections.content_md:
**Platform commission: NT keeps 20%, owner gets 80%.** When an owner asks "why is my payout less than the rental amount?" — that's the answer. 20% comes off the top.

**Payout flow:**
1. Owner ends the rental in their account (and uploads the 4 return photos)
2. NT initiates the payout
3. Funds land in the owner's bank in **1-3 business days** from initiation

Owners connect a bank account directly. **No Stripe Connect, no checks — direct bank deposit only.**

**Payout hold for DDC:** if the owner files a Damage Deposit Deduction Claim, the **payout is held until the claim is approved or denied.** The renter has a 24-hour dispute window from when the DDC is filed (see Module 6 for full mechanics). When dispute resolution completes, the payout (less any approved deductions) processes.

**The single biggest cause of payout failures is the owner's bank info — either not connected or entered incorrectly.** When an owner calls about a missing payout, ask them in this order:
1. Did you end the rental?
2. Is your bank account info connected and correct?
3. Is there a DDC pending on this booking?
4. How long has it been since the rental ended?

If bank info is the issue, walk them through correcting it. If everything looks right and it has been more than 5 business days with no DDC pending, escalate.
key_points: 20% platform commission — owner gets 80%
Payout: 1-3 business days from initiation, direct bank deposit only
DDC pending → payout held until claim resolved
Bank info is the #1 cause of payout failures
Triage: ended? bank info? DDC? how long?

sections.title: Early Return — Manual 80% Owner Refund
sections.content_md:
When a renter returns the trailer early, the **maximum refund is 80% of the rent for the unused portion** (per Section 20 of TOS — see Module 7 for detail).

Key CSR fact: **early-return refunds are MANUAL.** The owner has to approve and issue the refund through their account before ending the rental. **NT does not force owners to refund early returns**, and the refund does not auto-apply.

When a renter calls saying "I returned the trailer 2 days early — when does my refund come?" the answer is: "Early-return refunds aren't automatic. The owner authorizes them and chooses how much to refund, up to 80% of the unused portion. If the owner has approved one, it lands on your card in 3-5 business days."

**Be careful not to over-promise.** Don't tell a renter they're getting 80% back as if it's guaranteed. Frame it as: 80% is the cap; the owner decides whether to approve the refund and at what level.
key_points: Early-return refund is MANUAL, owner-issued — not automatic
80% of unused-portion rent is the CAP, not the default
Owner must authorize before ending the rental
Don't promise 80% as if guaranteed — frame as a cap

sections.title: Chargebacks
sections.content_md:
**NT ALWAYS fights chargebacks on behalf of the owner.** When a renter disputes a charge through their card issuer, NT files a response defending the booking.

**Outcome consequences:**
- If NT wins → the owner gets paid as normal.
- If NT loses → the owner does NOT get paid for that booking. The chargeback amount comes back from the owner's payout (or future payouts if already paid out).

**CSR's role when a chargeback is filed (or threatened):**
1. **Don't try to talk the customer out of the chargeback** — it's their right with their card issuer
2. **Gather documentation** — booking record, communications, the 4 start/end photos, any DDC paperwork
3. **File the chargeback response** with the available evidence
4. **Support the owner** through the process — explain timeline (chargeback resolution takes weeks), explain consequences
5. **Escalate** if the chargeback is unusually large or the documentation is thin

When a renter says "I'm going to chargeback," don't argue. Acknowledge the right, explain that NT will defend the booking through the chargeback response process, and let them know the resolution runs through NT and the card network.
key_points: NT always fights chargebacks on owner's behalf
If NT loses, the owner is NOT paid for that booking
CSR gathers documentation + files the response
Don't argue the customer out of a chargeback — it's their right

sections.title: The Toll Program
sections.content_md:
The Toll Program is an **opt-in feature for owners**. When enabled, NT auto-tracks tolls during rentals and bills the renter directly. This saves owners from chasing toll-by-mail bills weeks after the rental.

When explaining to owners, be honest about pros and cons:
- **Pro:** No more chasing toll-by-mail bills
- **Pro:** Renter pays automatically, no friction
- **Con:** Small percentage of edge cases where tolls aren't captured cleanly

Owners can opt in through their account settings. Encourage it for owners who rent frequently in toll-heavy areas.
key_points: Opt-in for owners
Auto-tracks tolls and bills the renter
Be honest about edge cases

modules.title: Deposits, Damage Claims & Disputes
modules.description: HIGH STAKES. The two-path damage claim flow, NT Protect coverage and limits, the DDC filing window, the renter's dispute window, and how to handle 'I'm being charged for damage I didn't cause.'
learning_objectives: Distinguish the two damage-claim paths: under-deposit DDC vs. over-deposit NT Protect claim
State NT Protect mechanics cold: $500 renter deductible, $25,000 coverage cap
Explain the DDC 48-hour filing window and the 24-hour renter dispute window
Apply the tire damage rule as case-by-case (condition + cause), not flat exclusion
Handle dispute calls without committing to fault, and know when CSR cancellation authority applies

sections.title: How Damage Claims Work — The Two-Path Flow
sections.content_md:
Damage claims have **two paths** and which one applies depends on the dollar amount versus the security deposit on file.

**Path 1 — Damage cost is at or under the deposit:** the **owner files a Damage Deposit Deduction Claim (DDC)** through the End Rental flow. The cost comes out of the renter's deposit. NT Protect is not invoked.

**Path 2 — Damage cost exceeds the deposit:** the owner first files the DDC for the deposit amount, then files an **NT Protect claim** for the remainder. NT Protect carries a **$500 renter deductible** and **caps at $25,000** per claim. The deductible normally comes out of the deposit. If the deductible cannot be collected from the renter, the **owner becomes responsible for the deductible**.

**A DDC must include four things:**
- A description specific to that rental (not generic wear-and-tear language)
- Photos showing the damage
- A repair estimate or invoice
- The completed claim form

**Filing window — DDC:** the claim must be filed **during the rental or within 48 hours after the rental ends**. Past 48 hours, there is no retroactive path. This is the highest-frequency owner-side mistake.

**Dispute window — renter:** once a DDC is filed, the renter has **24 hours** to dispute through their account. If the renter does not respond within 24 hours, the deduction **auto-approves** and processes against the deposit. This is the single largest urgency lever in dispute calls — when a renter calls in panicked about a charge, the timer matters.

**Deposit refund timing:** when no DDC is filed, the deposit refunds in **3-5 business days** after the rental closes. (When a DDC is filed, the deposit refund is held until the dispute window closes and the deduction processes.)
key_points: Two paths: under-deposit = DDC only; over-deposit = DDC + NT Protect claim
DDC filing window: during rental or up to 48 hours after
Renter dispute window: 24 hours, then auto-approves
Deposit refund: 3-5 business days when no DDC filed
DDC contents: description, photos, estimate, form

sections.title: NT Protect — Coverage, Deductible, Cap, and the Tire Rule
sections.content_md:
**NT Protect (Protection Package)** covers accidental damage during rentals when the cost exceeds the deposit. Two numbers every CSR must know cold:

- **$500 deductible** per claim (renter pays, typically from the deposit)
- **$25,000 maximum coverage** per claim

Both numbers are quotable to customers. Do not hedge or look these up on every call.

**What NT Protect DOES cover:**
- Accidental damage during a rental (dents, broken latches, fender damage, hail, accidental scratches during loading)
- Damage that exceeds the deposit (up to the $25k cap)

**What NT Protect does NOT cover:**
- **Intentional damage**
- **Theft due to renter negligence**
- Mechanical failure unrelated to the rental

**Tire damage is case-by-case — NOT a flat exclusion.** This is one of the highest-volume call types and the curriculum used to teach it wrong.

The rule is: **tire condition + cause** determine responsibility.
- **Renter pays** when good-condition tires are damaged by renter actions: impact (curbs, potholes), overload, excessive speed, road debris where the tire was sound.
- **Owner is responsible** when worn or old tires fail on their own: tread below safe depth, dry-rot, sidewall cracking, or otherwise defective tires that would have failed regardless of how the renter drove.

When a renter calls saying "I blew a tire on the highway, am I covered?" — the right answer is **"It depends on the tire's condition and what caused the failure. The owner files a claim, and NT reviews tire age, wear, and the cause of the blowout to determine responsibility."** Do not tell them flatly yes or flatly no.
key_points: $500 deductible, $25,000 cap — memorize both
Covers accidental damage; excludes intentional damage and theft from negligence
Tire damage is case-by-case (condition + cause), NOT a flat exclusion
Renter actions on good tires = renter pays. Worn/old tires that fail = owner's responsibility.
On tire calls, do NOT say "tires are excluded." Say "it depends on condition and cause."

sections.title: Handling Dispute Calls + CSR Cancellation Authority
sections.content_md:
**Dispute calls — the neutral-stance rule.** Never agree with a renter that 'the deduction is wrong' or that 'the owner is making it up.' You don't have visibility into the photos, the prior condition, or the repair estimate. Stay neutral and route them to the dispute process.

**What to say:**
- Acknowledge their frustration
- Explain the dispute process exists exactly for this situation
- Direct them to dispute through their account within the 24-hour window
- Tell them NT will review both sides and make a final determination

**What NOT to say:**
- 'That sounds unfair'
- 'I'll get this reversed for you'
- 'The owner is probably wrong'

If the customer demands a final answer or threatens chargeback, escalate.

**CSR cancellation authority — the nuance the old curriculum missed.**

The default rule is **renter self-service**: most cancellations should be done by the renter through their own account. Walk them through it.

**But CSRs DO have cancellation authority** for legitimate cases where self-service isn't appropriate. Use it sparingly. The legitimate triggers are:
- **Unresponsive owner** (booking not accepted in time, or owner has gone silent)
- **Suspected fraud** on either side of the booking
- **Renter emergency** with documentation (medical, family emergency)
- **Platform-side error** (system fault, listing pulled after booking)

When a CSR initiates the cancellation, NT chooses the **refund amount** (overrides the standard schedule when the situation warrants) and can **block the owner payout** while the case is reviewed. That's the whole point of CSR-initiated cancel — it's not just a courtesy; it's a tool that gives NT control over money flow.

**Bottom line:** default = self-service. Override path exists. Don't tell a customer 'I can't cancel for you' as a flat statement; the right phrasing is 'cancellations normally run through your account — let me walk you through it. If your situation falls under one of our exception cases, I can take it from here.'
key_points: Stay neutral on dispute calls; route to the 24-hour dispute window
Default cancellation flow = renter self-service through their account
CSR has override authority: unresponsive owner, fraud, renter emergency, platform error
CSR-initiated cancel lets NT choose the refund and block owner payout
Never tell a customer "I cannot cancel for you" as a flat statement

modules.title: Extensions, Cancellations & Late Returns
modules.description: Cancellation refund schedule, the 80% early-return override, extensions, and late-return escalation.
learning_objectives: Explain the cancellation refund schedule from memory
Explain the early return refund — including the 80% override
Walk renters and owners through extensions
Recognize a late return situation and trigger the right escalation

sections.title: Cancellation Refund Schedule
sections.content_md:
Memorize this:

| Time before rental start | Refund on rent |
|---|---|
| 72+ hours | 75% |
| 48-72 hours | 50% |
| 24-48 hours | 25% |
| Under 24 hours / same-day | 0% |

**Always non-refundable:** platform fee, tax, Protection Package.

**A no-show counts as a cancellation without notice = no refund.**

**Renter cancellation defaults to self-service through their account.** A phone, email, or chat request does NOT cancel a booking on its own. **However, CSRs DO have override authority** for legitimate exception cases (unresponsive owner, suspected fraud, renter emergency with documentation, platform-side error) — see Module 10, fact 5. Don't tell a customer "I cannot cancel for you" as a flat statement.

**Owner-side cancellation** is different: when an owner cancels and it's their fault, the owner pays a **30% fee from their next payout** (waivable for legitimate emergencies at NT discretion). See Module 4 for the full owner cancellation flow.
key_points: Platform fee, tax, Protection Package always non-refundable
No-show = no refund
Renter cancellation defaults to self-service; CSRs DO have override authority for legitimate exceptions
Owner cancellation = 30% fee from next payout (see Module 4)

sections.title: Early Return — The 80% Override
sections.content_md:
**This is the single most important policy correction in this curriculum.**

- Maximum refundable amount on early return is **80% of the rent for the unused portion** — per Section 20 of the Terms of Service.
- A **$25 processing fee comes out of the OWNER'S payout** — NOT from the renter's refund.
- The early-return refund is **MANUAL — owner has to approve and issue it before ending the rental.** It does not auto-apply. NT does not force owners to refund early returns.
- Refund processes back to the renter's card in **3-5 business days** (same as deposit and cancellation refunds).

**Known discrepancy:** The help center article currently says 75%. The correct policy cap is 80%. If a customer cites the 75% number, acknowledge that the help center text needs updating but the policy is 80%. If they press for documentation, escalate.

**Frame the 80% as a CAP, not a guarantee.** When a renter calls saying "I returned early — when's my refund?" the right answer is: "The owner authorizes early-return refunds and chooses how much, up to 80% of the unused portion. If they've approved one, it lands on your card in 3-5 business days." Don't promise 80% as if it's automatic.
key_points: Early return = up to 80% of rent for unused portion (CAP, not guarantee)
Manual — owner must authorize and issue before ending the rental
$25 fee comes from OWNER'S payout
Refund timing: 3-5 business days back to renter's card
Help center says 75% — that text is out of date

sections.title: Extensions
sections.content_md:
**Extension flow:**
1. **Renter** requests an extension through their account.
2. **Owner** approves (or declines) through their account.
3. **Pricing:** the extended period is billed at the **same daily rate** as the original rental.
4. **NT Protect coverage automatically extends with the rental** — there is no coverage cliff at the original end date.

**CSR scope:**
- **CSRs CANNOT initiate an extension request on behalf of the renter** — the renter has to do that themselves through their account.
- **CSRs CAN approve a pending extension on behalf of the owner** when the owner is unresponsive. This is the same kind of override authority that applies to unresponsive-owner cancellations.

**Restriction — cannot extend if another booking is queued.** If the trailer has a back-to-back booking immediately after, the system blocks the extension. Tell the renter: "There's another renter scheduled right after you, so the extension can't be approved. You'll need to return on time."

If the renter holds the trailer **beyond the booked end time without an approved extension, that's a late return** — see the next section.
key_points: Renter requests, owner approves; same daily rate as original
NT Protect auto-extends with the rental — no cliff
CSR cannot initiate an extension for the renter
CSR CAN approve a pending extension on behalf of an unresponsive owner
Cannot extend if another booking is queued for the trailer

sections.title: Late Returns
sections.content_md:
**Late returns are the OWNER'S responsibility to handle. NT does NOT auto-charge a late fee.**

When a renter holds the trailer past the booked end time without an approved extension, the owner has **two options**:
1. **Extend the rental** (with renter consent — see the extension flow above)
2. **File a Damage Deposit Deduction Claim (DDC)** and hold the deposit

There are also three communication templates the platform fires for escalating non-response:
1. **Late Return Notice** — first communication when trailer is overdue
2. **Urgent Notice** — escalated tone, more time has passed
3. **Trailer Not Returned by Renter** — final escalation before NT involvement

These templates **communicate** with the renter; they do not in themselves charge the renter. They lead toward the owner's choice between extending and filing a DDC. After 'Trailer Not Returned' has been sent and there is still no response, NT staff get involved — escalate.

**CSR talking points for the owner:**
- "Late returns are handled by you — the platform doesn't auto-charge a late fee."
- "You have two options: extend the rental if the renter agrees, or file a DDC and hold the deposit."
- "The Late Return / Urgent / Not Returned templates have already gone out (or will go out) to push the renter to respond."
- "If the renter goes truly silent past the final template, we escalate to staff."
key_points: Late returns are owner-handled — NT does not auto-charge a late fee
Owner's two options: extend (with renter consent) OR file a DDC
Three templates: Late Return Notice → Urgent Notice → Trailer Not Returned
Templates communicate; they do not charge
After final template + silence: NT staff escalation

modules.title: Phone Skills & Documentation
modules.description: This role is phone-heavy with backup email/chat support. Opening calls confidently, holds and transfers, the one-sentence response discipline, and the documentation requirement that applies to every interaction.
learning_objectives: Open inbound and outbound calls with the right structure
Handle holds, transfers, and closings
Maintain pace and tone — neither rushed nor rambling
Apply the 'one-sentence response' discipline: reactive, no upsells, no option dumps, no filler
Document every interaction in the right entity (profile / listing / booking) using the required format

sections.title: Channel Reality + Openings
sections.content_md:
**This role is phone-heavy.** Phone is the primary channel. Email and live chat are secondary backup channels you'll handle less frequently. The skills in this module apply to all three, but the default expectation is voice.

**Inbound opening:**
> 'Thanks for calling Neighbors Trailer, this is [name], how can I help you today?'

**Outbound opening:**
> 'Hi, this is [name] with Neighbors Trailer — is this [customer name]? Do you have a few minutes? I'm calling about [specific reason].'

Every outbound call must include three things: **who you are, where you're calling from, and the reason for the call.** Don't bury the lead.

After the opening, **wait for the customer to react.** Don't keep talking.
key_points: This role is phone-heavy; email/chat are secondary
Inbound: thanks for calling, name, how can I help
Outbound: name, NT, reason — all three
Wait for reaction

sections.title: Holds and Transfers
sections.content_md:
**Hold:**
> 'Can I put you on a brief hold while I look into that? I'll be back in under two minutes.'

Always come back when you said you would, even if it's just to give an update. If you said 30 seconds and it's been 90, come back and reset expectations.

**Transfer:**
> 'Let me transfer you to [team/person] who can help with that. One moment — I'll stay on the line until they pick up.'

Warm transfers (staying on until pickup) feel better to customers than cold transfers.
key_points: Hold timing: come back when you said you would
Transfer warmly — stay on until pickup
Set expectation, then meet it

sections.title: Closing & Tone Discipline
sections.content_md:
**Closing:**
> 'Is there anything else I can help you with today? Great — thanks for calling, have a good one.'

**Tone discipline (mirrors the Vapi voice agent):**
- **Concise.** Don't dump 5 options when 1 will do.
- **Reactive.** Wait for the customer to react before adding more info.
- **No filler.** Avoid 'um, like, kinda, basically,' and 'to be honest with you.'
- **No unsolicited upsells.** Don't pitch things they didn't ask about.
- **Don't read verbatim from the KB.** Paraphrase.

The one-sentence response discipline: when a customer asks a yes/no or simple question, answer it in one sentence. Then stop. Wait for the follow-up. Don't preempt questions they haven't asked.
key_points: Closing: anything else? thanks, have a good one
Concise > thorough
Reactive > preemptive
Paraphrase, don't read

sections.title: Documentation — Every Interaction, Every Time
sections.content_md:
**Every interaction must be documented in the system.** Phone, email, chat — doesn't matter. If you talked to a customer, it gets logged. This is most often forgotten on phone calls because there's no automatic transcript.

**Where to document:**
- **Profile** — for account-level interactions (verification questions, account access, password help)
- **Listing** — for listing-level interactions (approval status, photo issues, edits)
- **Booking** — for booking-level interactions (everything tied to a specific rental — the most common case)

Match the entity to what the call was about. A call about a renter's deposit goes on the booking. A call about an owner's bank info goes on the profile.

**Required format:** **date — initials — what was said/done.**

**Example:**
> `11/4/26 - LD - Spoke with renter, confirmed pickup address sent, no further action needed`

**Another example:**
> `11/4/26 - LD - Owner called re: payout 4 days late. Bank info confirmed correct. Escalated to payouts team.`

**Never skip documentation, even on "nothing happened" calls.** A renter who calls confused and you walked them through a self-serve fix is still a documented interaction. The next CSR who picks up that customer needs to see it.

The rule: **if it wasn't documented, it didn't happen.** Audit will treat undocumented interactions as a quality issue.
key_points: Document EVERY interaction — phone, email, chat
Where: profile (account-level) / listing (listing-level) / booking (booking-level — most common)
Format: date — initials — what was said/done
Don't skip "nothing happened" calls — the next CSR needs the trail
If it wasn't documented, it didn't happen

quiz.explanation: Who you are, where you're from (NT), and why you're calling. Always all three.

quiz.question: If you put a customer on hold for '30 seconds' and it's now been 90 seconds, what do you do?
options: Come back, give an update, set new expectation
Hang up and call them back
quiz.explanation: Come back when you said you would, even if it's just to update them.

quiz.question: Should you lead with a sales pitch on every inbound call?
options: Yes — every call is a chance to upsell
No — be reactive, no unsolicited pitches
Only on calls about the Toll Program
quiz.explanation: Be reactive. No unsolicited upsells. Answer what they asked.

quiz.question: What's the one-sentence response discipline?
quiz.explanation: Answer simple questions in one sentence, then stop and wait for the follow-up. Reactive, not preemptive.

quiz.question: You took a 4-minute phone call with a renter who just needed to be walked to their booking page to find the pickup address. Do you document?
options: No — only document calls that involve money or escalation
Yes — every interaction is documented, regardless of outcome
Only if the renter asks for a transcript
quiz.explanation: Every interaction is documented. "Nothing happened" calls still get a one-line entry. The next CSR picking up that customer needs the trail.

quiz.question: Required documentation format for every interaction?
quiz.explanation: Date — initials — what was said/done. Example: '11/4/26 - LD - Spoke with renter, confirmed pickup address sent, no further action needed.' Documentation lives on the profile, listing, or booking depending on what the call was about.

quiz.question: A call about an owner's bank info correction. Where does the documentation entry go?
options: On any listing — doesn't matter which
It doesn't need to be documented since it's a self-serve fix
quiz.explanation: Bank info is account-level → profile. Listing-level interactions go on the listing; booking-specific calls go on the booking.

modules.title: De-escalation & When to Escalate
modules.description: Spotting frustration, empathy without trashing the platform, the abuse-vs-anger judgment line, BBB threats (don't panic), chargeback threats (tiered authority), and knowing when to escalate.
learning_objectives: Spot early signs of a frustrated customer
Use empathy without over-apologizing or agreeing the policy is bad
Apply the abuse-vs-anger line: when can a CSR end an interaction unilaterally vs. escalate
Handle BBB threats without panicking or compromising on policy
Apply the tiered authority model on chargeback threats: new CSRs ask before bending; trusted CSRs can bend
Know exactly when to escalate vs. handle solo

sections.title: The De-escalation Framework
sections.content_md:
From the live chat bot rubric:
- **Be empathetic but balanced.**
- **Explain the purpose and importance** of processes (verification, compliance, etc.) rather than simply agreeing the experience is bad.
- **Always represent NT in a positive, professional light.**
- **Acknowledge the feeling, then redirect to the path forward.**

**Bad CSR move:**
> 'Yeah, that's so frustrating. Our system is awful, I'm so sorry.'

That agrees with the customer's negative framing of NT. Avoid this.

**Good CSR move:**
> 'I hear you — that wasn't the experience we want. The reason verification exists is to protect both renters and owners. Let me walk you through how to get past it.'

That acknowledges feeling, explains purpose, and redirects.
key_points: Empathy without trashing the platform
Explain the purpose of the process
Acknowledge → redirect → next step

sections.title: When to Escalate
sections.content_md:
Escalate when:
- Customer threatens **legal action**
- **Active payout failure** that requires admin tools (not a simple bank info fix)
- **Damage claim disputes** where the customer is demanding a final answer
- **Account suspension or compliance hold** inquiries
- **A specific booking ID where you don't have visibility**
- **Multi-topic situations** mixing billing + damage + escalated tone
- **You don't know.** When in doubt, escalate.

Do NOT escalate when:
- Standard how-to question (any KB-covered topic)
- Cancellation refund explanation
- Listing approval timing
- Toll Program questions
- Deposit refund timing (3-5 business days when no DDC is filed — see Module 5)

Customers asking for 'a manager' aren't an automatic escalation. Ask what they need; if it's something you can handle, handle it.
key_points: Always escalate: legal threats, payout admin issues, final-answer damage disputes, suspensions
Don't escalate: standard KB questions (deposit timing 3-5 days is a KB answer)
'I want a manager' ≠ escalate — ask what they need first

sections.title: Abuse vs. Anger — Where the Line Is
sections.content_md:
**This is judgment-based.** It's up to the CSR to recognize where frustration ends and abuse begins.

**Treat as anger (de-escalate, don't terminate):**
- Frustration with policy
- Raised voice
- Complaints about wait times, refund amounts, the process
- Repeated questions, persistent pushback

Work toward resolution. Don't match their tone.

**Treat as abuse (warn → end if it continues):**
- Personal attacks on the CSR
- Slurs
- Threats ("I'll find you," "I know where you work")
- Sustained profanity directed at the CSR (not just venting)

**Authority on clearly abusive interactions:**
- The CSR can **warn the customer** ("I want to help, but I can't continue if the language continues")
- If the abuse continues after the warning, the CSR can **end the interaction** — does not need manager approval for clearly abusive behavior
- Document the language used (verbatim if possible) in the system

**Borderline cases:** if you're not sure whether it's anger or abuse, **escalate to a manager** rather than terminating yourself. The judgment call exists exactly for cases where you're uncertain.

The underlying claim still gets evaluated on its merits — abusive language doesn't void a legitimate complaint. Process the issue separately from how the customer behaved.
key_points: Anger (frustration, raised voice, complaints) → de-escalate, don't terminate
Abuse (personal attacks, slurs, threats, sustained profanity at the CSR) → warn, then end if it continues
CSR can end clearly abusive interactions without manager approval
Borderline → escalate to manager rather than terminate yourself
Document the language. The underlying claim still gets evaluated on its merits.

sections.title: BBB Threats — Don't Panic
sections.content_md:
**BBB threats are common and they're not a huge concern operationally.** Don't change the answer just to avoid a BBB filing.

What to do:
- **Acknowledge the customer's right** to file with the BBB
- **Don't compromise on policy because of the threat** — the underlying issue is what matters, not the BBB filing
- **Handle the actual problem** the customer is calling about

What NOT to do:
- ❌ Issue a courtesy refund just because they mentioned the BBB
- ❌ Promise an exception to policy to avoid the threat
- ❌ Get defensive or anxious — the threat itself isn't the problem

**Example response:**
> 'I understand if you'd like to file with the BBB. That's your right. In the meantime, let me see what I can actually do about the underlying issue — let's go back to the deposit refund.'

The answer doesn't change because of the threat. Handle the underlying issue.
key_points: BBB threats are common — not operationally serious
Acknowledge the right to file; don't change the answer
Handle the underlying issue — that's what matters
Never issue a courtesy refund or waive a fee solely to avoid a BBB filing

sections.title: Chargeback Threats — Tiered Authority
sections.content_md:
**Chargeback threats are more serious than BBB threats.** A real chargeback costs the owner the booking (Module 5, §5-4). Take them seriously, but don't panic.

**CSR's first move:** de-escalate and understand the underlying complaint. Most chargeback threats come from a real grievance that can be addressed without bending policy.

**Tiered authority model — read this carefully:**

**If you are NEW or junior:**
- **ASK FOR APPROVAL before bending policy.** Do NOT solo-decide to issue a courtesy refund or waive a fee.
- Escalate the situation; let the manager decide whether a compromise is warranted.
- This is not a sign of weakness — it's the right move when you're still building judgment about which compromises are appropriate.

**Once you are trained and trusted as a decision-maker:**
- You can **bend rules in chargeback-threat situations** to prevent the chargeback:
  - Issue a courtesy refund (even if policy doesn't require it)
  - Waive or reduce a fee
  - Other compromise positions
- But: **do NOT compromise on serious policy issues just to prevent a chargeback.** A chargeback threat doesn't license you to override damage claim findings, MVR rejections, or compliance holds.

**The framing:** "When you're new, ask. When you're trusted, here are the tools you have." The authority grows with experience and judgment — it isn't a binary CSR-can or CSR-can't.

**Always:** gather documentation regardless. If the chargeback gets filed, NT defends the booking through the chargeback response process. The documentation you gather now becomes the response material later.
key_points: Chargeback threats are more serious than BBB threats — but don't panic
New/junior CSRs: ASK before bending policy — don't solo-decide a courtesy refund
Trained/trusted CSRs: can issue courtesy refunds, waive fees, find compromises
Never compromise on serious policy (damage findings, MVR, compliance) just to prevent a chargeback
Always gather documentation — feeds the chargeback response if filed

quiz.explanation: Legal threats are an automatic escalation.

quiz.explanation: Ask what they need first. If it's something a CSR can handle, handle it. Only escalate if it's a real escalation trigger.

options: Wait for a manager to authorize ending the call

options: Issue a courtesy refund to avoid the filing

quiz.explanation: BBB threats are common and not operationally serious. Acknowledge the right to file, don't compromise on policy, handle the underlying issue. Never issue a courtesy refund just to avoid a BBB filing.

quiz.question: You're a new CSR. A renter threatens to chargeback if you don't waive a $50 fee. What do you do?
options: Waive the fee on the spot to prevent the chargeback

Tell them to go ahead and file — chargebacks always lose
Refuse to engage with the threat
quiz.explanation: New/junior CSRs ASK before bending policy. Don't solo-decide a courtesy refund or fee waiver. Once trained and trusted, the CSR can bend rules to prevent a chargeback — but not while still building judgment.

modules.title: Critical Policy Knowledge
modules.description: The 15 must-know facts every CSR must know cold. Spaced-repetition drilling required before unlocking the final exam.
learning_objectives: Recall all 15 critical facts from memory in any order
Apply each fact correctly in a customer scenario
Recognize when the help center contradicts the curriculum (and follow the curriculum)

sections.title: The 15 Facts
sections.content_md:
Every CSR must know these cold. The drill in this module quizzes you in random order until you get all 15 right two times in a row.

1. **Early return refund max = 80%** of rent for the unused portion. Per Section 20 of TOS. The help center 75% text is out of date.
2. **The $25 early-return processing fee comes from the OWNER'S payout.** Not from the renter's refund.
3. **Platform fees, tax, and Protection Package are non-refundable** once a booking is accepted.
4. **Cancellation refund schedule:** 72+ hrs = 75% / 48-72 hrs = 50% / 24-48 hrs = 25% / under 24 hrs = 0% / same-day = 0%.
5. **Cancellations default to renter self-service through their account. CSRs DO have override authority** for legitimate cases (unresponsive owner, suspected fraud, renter emergency with documentation, platform-side error). CSR-initiated cancel lets NT choose the refund and block owner payout. Use sparingly. Never tell a customer "I cannot cancel for you" as a flat statement.
6. **A no-show = no refund** (treated as a renter cancellation without notice).
7. **Deposit refund timing: 3-5 business days** after the rental is officially closed (when no DDC is filed). Same window for cancellation and early-return refunds.
8. **Tire damage is case-by-case.** Tire condition + cause determine responsibility. Worn/old tires that fail on their own = owner. Good-condition tires damaged by renter actions (impact, overload, speed) = renter. NOT a flat exclusion.
9. **CSR cannot override an MVR rejection.** Escalate.
10. **NT Protect: $500 renter deductible, $25,000 coverage cap per claim.** Both numbers are quotable to customers. If the deductible cannot be collected from the renter, the owner becomes responsible for it.
11. **DDC filing window: during the rental or within 48 hours after.** Past 48 hours = no retroactive claim path.
12. **Renter dispute window after a DDC is filed = 24 hours.** No response in 24 hours = auto-approve and the deduction processes.
13. **Pickup address is shared automatically after owner acceptance** — most 'where's my address' calls are renters who haven't checked their booking page.
14. **Phone number policy: do not proactively share NT's phone number** unless the customer explicitly asks. Default to email or live chat.
15. **Owner cancellation = 30% fee deducted from next payout.** No "with cause / without cause" terminology. Edge cases (legitimate emergency, trailer broke unexpectedly) waivable at NT discretion — open the door to a waiver review, don't promise one.
key_points: 15 facts — must know cold
When help center contradicts curriculum, follow curriculum
Drill randomized — 2x in a row to pass

sections.title: Why These Fifteen
sections.content_md:
Each of these facts represents one of:
- A **policy correction** the help center got wrong (early return, deposit timing, tire damage)
- A **scope boundary** for what a CSR can and can't do — including the override authority CSRs DO have (cancellations, MVR, extension approval)
- A **commonly-misunderstood timing** (deposit refund, DDC window, dispute window, pickup address)
- A **friction point** that drives high call volume (cancellation schedule, NT Protect numbers, owner cancellation fee)

Knowing them cold means most calls become muscle memory instead of KB lookups. Several of these are the answers to the highest-volume questions in the entire support queue — every minute spent looking these up on a call is a minute the customer is on hold.
key_points: Policy corrections, scope boundaries (with override authority), timing, friction points
Cold knowledge = faster calls, fewer mistakes

modules.title: Final Certification Exam
modules.description: 25 questions sampled across all modules. Pass = 84% (21/25). Fail = remediation on lowest-scoring modules.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate end-to-end CSR competence across all modules
Apply policies correctly under realistic scenario pressure
Recognize escalation triggers correctly

sections.title: Exam Instructions
sections.content_md:
**Format:** 25 questions sampled across Modules 1-10. Questions come from the exam-questions pool.

**Passing score:** 84% (21 of 25 correct).

**Rules:**
- No AI tutor available during the exam.
- Multi-choice questions are auto-scored.
- Short-answer questions are graded by Claude against the rubric.
- You can take the exam multiple times if you fail. After failing, the system will recommend remediation on your lowest-scoring modules.

**Scoring rubric (for short-answer questions):**
- Did you identify the correct policy/procedure?
- Did you communicate it clearly and concisely?
- Did you handle de-escalation appropriately when the situation called for it?
- Did you correctly recognize escalation triggers?
- Did you avoid sharing the phone number unless explicitly asked?
- Did you paraphrase rather than reading verbatim from the KB?

**Pass = certified** to start handling live customers with backup support.
key_points: 25 questions, 84% to pass
Auto-scored multi-choice + Claude-graded short-answer
No AI tutor during exam
Retakes allowed with remediation recommendations
